query: effect of amblyopia treatment on subfoveal choroidal thickness

retrieved_docs.title: Amblyopia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Research conducted using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) and optical coherence tomography (OCT) in individuals with amblyopia have revealed neuroanatomical structural and functional alterations in the gray matter volume of the corresponding visual cortex hemispheres, [40] alteration in cortical thickness within several white matter volumes, [41] variations in ciliary muscle thickness, [42] changes in choroidal thickness, [43] differences in retinal nerve fiber thickness, [44] variations in central macular thickness, [45] and modifications in other structures along the visual pathway. [46] These findings highlight the extensive impact of amblyopia on both the structural and functional aspects of the visual system.

retrieved_docs.title: Optical Coherence Tomography -- Technique or Treatment -- Measuring Choroidal Thickness Using Spectral-Domain Optical Coherence Tomography
retrieved_docs.content: Choroidal thickness measurement with SD-OCT requires specialized training and should only be performed by a qualified eye care professional. Pupillary dilatation can temporarily increase choroidal thickness and negatively affect the accuracy of choroidal thickness measurements; choroidal thickness should be measured before any dilatation is performed. Accurate choroidal thickness measurements require the zero-delay line to be set as close as possible to the choroid without touching it.

retrieved_docs.title: The Conoid of Sturm -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: History – Habitual squinting is an attempt to reduce the size of the circle of most minor confusion on the retina. A history of amblyopia, corneal disease, or cataracts can cause astigmatism. A family history of keratoconus or certain corneal dystrophies can increase the suspicion of astigmatism.

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Length -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Focal length calculations are important when managing common eye conditions such as cataracts and evaluating patients for refractive surgical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Myopic Foveoschisis  -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of surgery depends on the patient's preoperative visual acuity. The lower the visual acuity, the poorer the surgical outcome. If the visual symptoms have been present for a long time, the likelihood of complete visual recovery is remote. The presence of foveal detachment and a full-thickness macular hole worsens the prognosis. High axial length, deep staphyloma, and the presence of choroidal thinning all make the surgical outcome less favorable, with poor recovery of visual acuity and incomplete anatomical resolution. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Childhood Myopia and Ocular Development -- Treatment / Management -- 7 Methylxanthine
retrieved_docs.content: 7-Methylxanthine is a metabolite of theobromine and caffeine. The possible mechanism of action of the drug is to modulate the axial length by increasing the collagen fibril diameters and overall thickness of the posterior sclera. [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Myopic Foveoschisis  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Myopic foveoschisis, also referred to as myopic traction maculopathy or myopic macular schisis, is a rare condition occurring in patients with pathological myopia. This condition results from an increase in the size of the eyeball, leading to stretching and separation of the retinal and choroidal layers. This condition typically manifests as a gradual, progressive, and painless loss of vision in one or both eyes. Early identification and management are critical to mitigating its impact on a patient's quality of life and long-term health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: choroid scar all represent reparative or fibrotic sequelae that localize to the choroidal layer after injury or inflammation. Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), and secondary spread of choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), reflecting age‑related or progressive loss of choroidal structure and function. Traumatic disruptions of the choroid include choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي) and choroidal inversion (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while fluid‑related detachments are described as serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية) and general choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 978
retrieved_docs.content: The choroid (choroidea) is the principal anatomic substrate for a wide spectrum of ocular diseases, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, traumatic, postoperative and structural lesions. Inflammatory involvement includes disseminated choroiditis, combined disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, as well as the Arabic‑language entities التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية (retino‑choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري (focal choroid‑retinal inflammation) and unspecified variants. Traumatic, postoperative and post‑inflammatory processes give rise to scarring such as post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, post‑traumatic choroid scar, and disseminated scar of retina and choroid; the term “litteken; choroidea” also denotes a choroidal scar. Age‑related degeneration is represented by الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية (senile choroidal atrophy). Structural disruptions include التمزق المشيمي (choroidal tear) and serous detachments such as انفصال المشيمية المصلية (serous choroidal detachment) and انفصال مشيمية العين (choroidal detachment of the eye). Additional choroidal alterations comprise تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal inversion), حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي (partial choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula), حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي (total choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula), and ندبة مشيمية شبكية (chorioretinal scar). Collectively, these entities underscore the central role of the choroid in ocular pathology and highlight the importance of recognizing choroidal involvement for accurate diagnosis and effective management of diverse eye diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1158

Structural and degenerative changes include age‑related choroidal atrophy (“الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية”), secondary choroidal atrophy spread (“انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية”), partial peripapillary choroidal sclerosis (“حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي”), and choroidal inversion or folding (“تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة”). Traumatic and reparative sequelae are represented by choroidal tear (“التمزق المشيمي”), choroidal detachment (“انفصال مشيمية العين”), post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar. Scarring that involves both retina and choroid is noted as disseminated scar of retina and choroid, while “litteken; choroidea” and retinal‑choroidal scar (“ندبة مشيمية شبكية”) denote isolated choroidal scar tissue. The choroidea also serves as the direct procedural site for therapeutic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is a principal anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular diseases, serving as the locus for hemorrhagic events (نزيف مشيمي – choroidal hemorrhage), tears (التمزق المشيمي – choroidal tear), atrophic changes (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية – senile choroidal atrophy; انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية – secondary choroidal atrophy spread; تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة – choroidal atrophy; تنكس حصوي – calcific degeneration), serous and hemorrhagic detachments (انفصال المشيمية المصلية – serous choroidal detachment; انفصال مشيمية العين – choroidal detachment of the eye; انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي – hemorrhagic choroidal detachment), and sclerotic lesions (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي – partial choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي – total choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula). A wide array of inflammatory and infectious processes also involve the choroidea, including Disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, Focal choroiditis, Generalized disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Juxtapapillary focal choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, and unspecified widespread retino‑choroiditis (الالتهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد). Post‑inflammatory and traumatic sequelae frequently produce scarring (Postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, Postinflammatory choroid scar, Postsurgical choroid scar, Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, Posttraumatic choroid scar, ندبة مشيمية شبكية – chorioretinal scar). The choroidea is also linked to بُقَعُ روث. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s central role in ocular disease assessment, diagnosis, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1160
retrieved_docs.content: indicating a diffuse loss of choroidal tissue integrity. Peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis (Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis) illustrate localized inflammatory lesions at the retinal‑choroidal interface. Choroidal detachments are also catalogued as choroidal findings. Serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي) describe separations of the choroid from the sclera due to fluid or blood accumulation. Reversal of the choroid (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) denotes a positional change of the tissue, often secondary to severe hypotony or trauma. Finally, metabolic and hereditary deposits can affect the choroid. Partial peripupillary choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect abnormal calcium accumulation within the choroidal stroma. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2392
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) serves as the anatomic location for a wide spectrum of ocular pathologies. Disseminated choroiditis, disseminated scar of the retina and choroid, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar are all conditions in which the choroidea is identified as the finding site. In addition, the choroidea is linked to specific lesion descriptors such as “التمزق المشيمي” (choroidal tear), “التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد” (unspecified disseminated chorioretinitis), “التهاب المشيمية” (choroiditis), “التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد” (unspecified focal choroiditis and retinitis), “انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية” (secondary choroidal atrophy), “انفصال المشيمية المصلية” and “انفصال مشيمية العين” (serous choroidal detachment), and “ندبة مشيمية شبكية” (retinal‑choroidal scar). Collectively, these relationships underscore the choroidea’s central role as the site of inflammatory, degenerative, traumatic, and postoperative ocular findings, highlighting its importance in diagnostic evaluation and management of a variety of chorioretinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: حصوي (calcific degeneration of choroidal or retinal tissue), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal atrophy) and حثل المشيمية حول الحُليلة، جزئي (partial sclerosis of the choroid surrounding the macula). Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the principal anatomic locus for a broad spectrum of ocular disorders, including hemorrhagic, inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, postoperative, and metastatic conditions. Hemorrhagic involvement is exemplified by choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي). Inflammatory diseases affecting the choroid encompass disseminated choroiditis, disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, focal choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, familial choroidal lesions (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and focal, unspecified choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد). Degenerative processes include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, stone‑like choroidal atrophy (تنكس حصوي), general choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), and peri‑optic choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي). Post‑inflammatory sequelae

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.
